title: The Hijra — Muhammad's migration from Mecca to Medina
summary: In the summer of 622 the Prophet Muhammad and his small band of followers slipped out of Mecca and travelled north across the Hijaz to the oasis town of Yathrib, soon renamed Medina. The Hijra, or emigration, ended more than a decade of persecution by the Quraysh and gave the new Muslim community a territory of its own. Within a generation the year of the Hijra would become the starting point of the Islamic calendar and the founding moment of a new political and religious order.

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
For twelve years Muhammad ibn Abd Allah had preached a rigorously monotheistic message in his native Mecca, a caravan city built around the Kaaba shrine and dominated by the polytheistic Quraysh confederation to which he himself belonged. His warnings of a coming judgement, his rejection of the ancestral idols, and his insistence that rich and poor stand equal before God threatened both the religious prestige of the Quraysh and the pilgrimage economy on which their wealth rested. From about 613, when Muhammad began to preach openly, opposition hardened. Followers of low status, among them the freed Ethiopian slave Bilal, were tortured; the Prophet's own clan, the Banu Hashim, was subjected to a commercial boycott between roughly 616 and 619 that drove them into a mountain quarter of the city and cost several lives.

In 619, a year later remembered by Muslims as the Year of Sorrow, Muhammad lost both his wife Khadija and his uncle and clan protector Abu Talib. Without Abu Talib's shield the Prophet was newly exposed, and his tentative appeal to the neighbouring city of Taif was rejected humiliatingly. He began looking further afield. During the annual pilgrimage seasons at Mecca he approached delegations from tribes across Arabia. Two years running, in 620 and 621, small groups of the Khazraj tribe of Yathrib listened and accepted his message. In 622 a larger party of Aws and Khazraj representatives, some seventy-five men and two women, met him secretly at Aqaba outside Mecca and swore what tradition calls the Second Pledge of Aqaba: they promised to defend him as they would defend their own kin.

Yathrib, more than four hundred kilometres north of Mecca, was a rambling agricultural oasis of date palms, mud-brick strongholds and rival tribal quarters. Its Arab tribes, the Aws and Khazraj, had exhausted themselves in a long feud that had culminated at the battle of Buath around 618. Its three Jewish tribes, the Banu Qaynuqa, Banu Nadir and Banu Qurayza, were prosperous but internally divided. Muhammad's role as a trusted arbitrator from outside the local blood feuds was in many ways precisely what a war-weary town needed. Through the spring and summer of 622 small groups of Meccan Muslims quietly sold their houses and travelled north; by midsummer perhaps seventy families had already reached the oasis, ready to receive their teacher.

sections.heading: What happened
sections.body:
The Quraysh leadership grew alarmed as the Meccan Muslim community drained away. According to the seerah tradition preserved by Ibn Ishaq in the eighth century, an emergency council at the Dar al-Nadwa resolved that a young man from each Quraysh clan should strike Muhammad down together, so that no single family could be held responsible in blood. Warned of the plot, Muhammad determined to leave. He entrusted the goods people had deposited with him, on the strength of his old nickname al-Amin, the trustworthy, to his young cousin Ali ibn Abi Talib and asked him to sleep in his bed to deceive the watchers outside.

On a night in June 622 Muhammad slipped out of the house with his closest companion Abu Bakr and made not northwards but south into the hills. For three days the pair hid in a cave on the slopes of Mount Thawr while Quraysh trackers scoured the caravan tracks; a spider's web and a nesting rock dove at the cave mouth, in the pious tradition, persuaded the searchers that no one had entered. Abu Bakr's daughter Asma brought them food; the Bedouin guide Abd Allah ibn Uraiqit led them by an obscure western route along the coast of the Red Sea. Even now the danger was not over: a hunter named Suraqa ibn Malik, tempted by a bounty of a hundred camels, briefly caught up with them before turning back.

On 12 Rabi al-Awwal by later reckoning, corresponding to 24 September 622 in the Julian calendar, Muhammad reached Quba on the southern edge of Yathrib, where he waited for Ali and then rode into the oasis to a jubilant welcome. Muslim historians say he let his camel, Qaswa, choose the site of the first mosque, which was built of palm trunks and mud brick beside the modest houses of the Prophet and his wives. Within months, Muhammad had drafted the document conventionally called the Constitution of Medina, which bound the Muhajirun (the Meccan emigrants), the Ansar (the Aws and Khazraj helpers) and the Jewish tribes of the oasis into a single umma, or community, with the Prophet as final arbiter of disputes. Yathrib became al-Madina al-Munawwara, the enlightened city.

sections.heading: Aftermath & consequences
sections.body:
The transformation was immediate. In Mecca Muhammad had been the leader of a persecuted sect; in Medina he was, within two years, the effective head of state of a substantial oasis polity, with the power to legislate, to command in war and to make treaties with neighbouring tribes. The old Meccan revelations, meditative and warning in tone, gave way in Medina to longer suras dealing with marriage, inheritance, the ritual pilgrimage, alms and jihad. The Kaaba at Mecca, initially eclipsed by Jerusalem as the direction of prayer, was restored as the qibla in 624, a decisive symbolic break with Jewish and Christian precedent.

Within two years the Muslims of Medina had begun raiding Quraysh caravans on the trade road to Syria, and in March 624 a small Muslim force under Muhammad himself won a startling victory at Badr against a much larger Meccan army. Defeats and setbacks followed, notably at Uhud in 625 and the siege of Medina in 627, but the political initiative had passed. The Meccan aristocracy's grip on the Hijaz weakened as their trade suffered, and in January 630 Muhammad returned to his birthplace at the head of ten thousand men and accepted its almost bloodless surrender. The idols around the Kaaba were smashed, and the city was formally incorporated into the Muslim polity.

Under the second caliph Umar ibn al-Khattab, some seventeen years after the emigration, a decision was taken that gave the Hijra its lasting cosmic importance. Faced with the administrative chaos of dating documents in a new empire that had swallowed Persia and half of Byzantium, Umar convened a council that ruled the year of the Hijra to be Year 1. Because Arabian months began at Muharram, the new era was backdated to 16 July 622 in the Julian calendar. Every Islamic document, coin and tombstone from that day forward, from the Atlantic to Central Asia, would be dated AH, Anno Hegirae, in the year of the Hijra.

sections.heading: Legacy
sections.body:
For Muslims the Hijra is not primarily a flight but a founding. The Prophet's own biographers, and later scholars from al-Tabari in the tenth century to Ibn Kathir in the fourteenth, presented the migration as the pivot between the era of preaching and the era of the community, between private religion and public order. The pledges of Aqaba became the model for later oaths of allegiance to caliphs; the Constitution of Medina, whatever its exact original wording, was invoked in the twentieth century by figures as varied as Muhammad Iqbal and the drafters of national constitutions in Pakistan and beyond as evidence that Islam had always understood pluralism.

The Hijri calendar has organised Muslim time ever since. Because it is strictly lunar, its year is roughly eleven days shorter than the solar year, so the fast of Ramadan and the pilgrimage of Dhu al-Hijja migrate slowly through the seasons. In the Iranian world a solar Hijri calendar, dating from the same event but keyed to the vernal equinox, is still the civil calendar of Iran and Afghanistan. In many countries, Hijri dates share stamps, gravestones and government letterheads with the Gregorian.

Beyond religion the Hijra shaped a political vocabulary. To make hijra came to mean, in Islamic law, to leave a land of oppression for a land where the faith could be practised freely, an idea invoked by African-American Muslims in the twentieth century, by Bosnians fleeing the wars of the 1990s, and by countless groups since. The migration from Mecca to Medina, an obscure escape by a handful of desert townsfolk in the summer of 622, thus became one of the most consequential journeys in world history, generating a civilisation, an empire, a calendar and a language of political hope.

worldContext.text: The Eastern Roman emperor Heraclius, exhausted by a decade of losing wars against the Sasanian Persians, was preparing the counter-offensive that would eventually recover Jerusalem in 630. Constantinople itself would be besieged by Avars and Persians in 626. In the west, Visigothic Spain had recently completed its conversion from Arianism to Nicene Christianity, and the Merovingian Frankish kingdoms of Chlothar II were consolidating around Paris. Italy remained divided between Byzantine provinces and a patchwork of Lombard duchies.

In China the young Tang dynasty, founded by Li Yuan only four years earlier, was still fighting rival warlords. In 626 the crown prince Li Shimin would murder his brothers at the Xuanwu Gate incident and take the throne as Emperor Taizong, inaugurating the long reign that carried Tang power into Central Asia. In Japan the reforms of the recently deceased Prince Shotoku were being consolidated by Empress Suiko's regents, and Korean politics were dominated by the rivalry of Goguryeo, Baekje and Silla.

Northern India was ruled by the emperor Harsha from his capital at Kannauj, one of the last great pan-Indian sovereigns before the medieval fragmentation. The Buddhist university at Nalanda was preparing to receive the Chinese pilgrim Xuanzang, who would spend years there in the 630s. In the Deccan the Chalukyas of Badami under Pulakeshin II were extending their power, and along the Silk Road the Western Turk Khaganate still controlled the passes into Sogdiana.

The Christian kingdom of Aksum, then ruled by King Armah, had recently sheltered a small group of Muslim refugees sent by Muhammad himself from Mecca in what tradition calls the First Hijra to Abyssinia around 615. In Nubia the kingdoms of Nobatia and Makuria were consolidating around a Miaphysite Christianity linked to Alexandria. In North Africa Byzantine exarchs still ruled from Carthage over a countryside where Berber confederations were reasserting local power.

In the Maya lowlands the Classic period was at its dazzling height. Palenque was ruled by K'inich Janaab Pakal, then a small child, who would go on to reign for nearly seventy years and commission the celebrated tomb at the Temple of the Inscriptions. Tikal and Calakmul were locked in the great regional rivalry that would define seventh-century Maya politics. In the central Andes the Wari state was extending its terraced administrative centres across the highlands, while the Tiwanaku empire dominated the Lake Titicaca basin.

figures.name: Muhammad
figures.role: Prophet of Islam; led the emigration from Mecca in 622 and became religious and political head of the Medinan community until his death in 632

figures.name: Abu Bakr
figures.role: Muhammad's closest companion and companion on the Hijra journey; later the first caliph after Muhammad's death

figures.name: Ali ibn Abi Talib
figures.role: Muhammad's young cousin; slept in the Prophet's bed to deceive the Quraysh assassins on the night of the Hijra

figures.name: Umar ibn al-Khattab
figures.role: Second caliph, from 634 to 644; instituted the Hijri calendar and backdated it to Year 1 = 622 CE

images.caption: The Cave of Hira on Jabal al-Nur outside Mecca, where Muhammad received his first revelations. The nearby Cave of Thawr sheltered him and Abu Bakr on the first days of the Hijra.
A schematic map of the traditional route of the Hijra from Mecca to Medina, following the western coastal tracks of the Hijaz.
